title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Re-anchoring: the bridge to the Car Project + meet electric charge
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — From waves to circuits

Unit 7 is the last theory unit before Unit 8's Car Project. Every part of that build — battery, switch, motor, wiring — is in this unit.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (preview)

K1. Electric charge as the fundamental quantity. R1. Why opposite charges attract; like repel.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Where we are: end of Unit 6. Radar told us the asteroid. Now Unit 7 closes the theory year by getting us to electrical+magnetic phenomena AT planetary AND human scales.

Today's pivot: Two threads converge in Unit 7. (1) Earth's magnetosphere protects us from cosmic + solar radiation — large impacts CAN disrupt it. (2) Unit 8's Car Project literally builds a circuit. Today through Day 15: the foundations. Day 16: asteroid/cosmic E&M. Day 17: the explicit bridge into the Car Project.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Return Unit 6 transfer tasks. Highlight strong radar reasoning. "Now we trade waves for charges. Two weeks of theory, then we BUILD."
5–18 min

• Demo: rub a balloon on hair. Stick to wall. Why? Then: tear paper into bits, sprinkle. Bring the rubbed balloon near. Bits jump up. Force without contact. What's happening?
18–28 min

• Notice & Wonder. Push toward: there's a PROPERTY (call it charge) that comes in TWO KINDS, and they pull on each other through space. Don't define yet — just name it.
28–42 min

• Mini-lecture preview: Unit 7's payoffs. (1) Days 4-10: circuits — voltage, current, resistance, series + parallel — everything the Car Project needs. (2) Days 12-15: magnetism + electromagnetism — Oersted, Faraday, motors. (3) Day 16: planetary-scale E&M (magnetosphere + EMP scenarios). (4) Day 17: explicit bridge into Unit 8.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "You rub two balloons on your hair, then bring them near each other. Do they attract or repel? Why?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1 preview.

Prep: Unit 6 transfer tasks scored; balloons + hair (hand sanitizer can help when hair fails); torn paper bits; whiteboard with two columns for student observations.

Talk moves on "why two kinds of charge": resist explaining. Push: "What would happen if all charges were the same kind? Could anything ever attract or stick?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing balloon-on-hair triboelectric pattern with +/- annotated; SWDs — verbal exit ticket option; advanced — "could you have a UNIVERSE with one kind of charge instead of two? Why does ours have two?"

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — Coulomb's law: F = kq₁q₂/r²
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — The electrical force law

F = k·q₁q₂/r². Inverse-square law for charges, structurally identical to gravity.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K2. Coulomb's law structure. S1. Compute Coulomb force.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Compare Coulomb's law (F = k·q₁q₂/r²) to Newton's universal gravitation (F = G·m₁m₂/r²). Same structure. Different constants, different scaled outcomes. Both inverse-square. Why does physics keep doing this?

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hand back Day 1 exit tickets. Anchor: "Today we get the equation."
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: F = k·q₁q₂/r². k = 9×10⁹ N·m²/C². Units of q = Coulombs (C). Compare to Newton's gravity — SAME STRUCTURE. But k is HUGE (gravity's G is tiny) → electrical force is much stronger at small distances.
18–30 min

• Worked example 1: F between two 1-C charges 1 m apart. F = 9×10⁹ N. Stunning number. Why we never see 1 C of net charge in everyday life.
30–42 min

• Worked example 2: F between two electrons (q = -1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C each) at 10⁻¹⁰ m (atomic scale). Compare to gravitational F between two electron masses at the same distance. Electrical is ~10⁴² times stronger.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "If you DOUBLE the distance between two charges, what happens to F?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K2/S1.

Prep: packet practice; calculator; reference table comparing gravity vs. Coulomb constants.

Talk moves on "why we never see 1 C of charge": push: "What kind of force WOULD it take to assemble 1 C? Why does electrostatic repulsion fight against you?"

Differentiation: ELs — equation card with each piece labeled; SWDs — pre-built table for the two-side-by-side comparison (gravity vs. Coulomb); advanced — "compute the ratio of Coulomb to gravitational force between two protons. Why is gravity dominant at galaxy scales but Coulomb dominant at human scales?"

title: Day 3 · Phase 1 — Electric field (qualitative)
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 1 — The field around a charge

E = F/q. Field lines point from + to −. Vector field that fills space around charges.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. Electric field as F per unit charge. Field-line direction conventions.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Just like Unit 2's gravitational field g(r), today we define E(r) — the electric field around a charge. The field is the FRAMEWORK both gravity and electricity share.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap Coulomb's law: F = k·q₁q₂/r².
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: divide both sides by q_test. Define E = F/q. Units: N/C. The electric field is the FORCE PER UNIT CHARGE that a tiny positive test charge would feel.
18–30 min

• Field lines: arrows pointing in the direction of force on a positive test charge. Field lines START on + charges and END on − charges. Diagram on the board: single +, single −, dipole, parallel plates.
30–42 min

• PhET Charges and Fields sim: place a charge, watch the field. Add another. See the dipole pattern. Place a test charge and watch it move.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Draw the field lines for a single negative charge. Which way do they point?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K3.

Prep: PhET Charges and Fields loaded on lab computers; field-line drawing template for the board.

Talk moves on "field lines point AWAY from + and TOWARD −": when students get confused, push: "What direction would a tiny POSITIVE charge be pushed? That's the direction the lines point."

Differentiation: ELs — pre-drawn field-line patterns with direction arrows; SWDs — drawing template for single +/− and dipole; advanced — "explain why field lines NEVER cross. Hint: what would happen to a test charge at a crossing?"

title: Day 4 · Phase 2 — Voltage: electrical potential difference
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 2 — Voltage is energy per charge

V = U/q. Volts = Joules/Coulomb. Voltage is what PUSHES current through a wire.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. Voltage as potential difference.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The Car Project uses a 9V battery. The voltage is what pushes current through the motor. Today we untangle what "voltage" really means.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: "AA battery is 1.5 V. Car battery is 12 V. House outlet is 120 V. What does the number MEAN?"
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: V = U/q. Voltage is energy per unit charge. 1 V = 1 J of energy delivered per 1 C of charge that passes. The voltage of a battery tells you how much PUSH (energy per charge) it provides.
18–30 min

• Water analogy: voltage = water pressure. Tall tank (high voltage) pushes water harder than short tank. The battery is like a pump that keeps the "water" (charge) pushed up to a height.
30–42 min

• Worked examples: (1) 9V battery delivers 1.5 C of charge through a motor. How much energy delivered? (13.5 J) (2) How much charge has to flow through a 1.5V battery to deliver 1 J of energy? (0.67 C)
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A battery does 60 J of work pushing 5 C of charge through a circuit. What's the battery voltage?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K4.

Prep: AA + 9V + lithium-ion battery props; water-tank analogy diagram; packet practice; calculator.

Talk moves on the water analogy: push: "What's the analog of CURRENT in the water analogy? What's RESISTANCE?" (Preview tomorrow.)

Differentiation: ELs — analogy card (voltage = pressure, current = flow, resistance = pipe-narrowness); SWDs — pre-built unit-conversion practice (V → J/C and back); advanced — "why does a 9V battery NOT shock you, but a 120V outlet might? Voltage isn't the only factor — preview tomorrow."

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — Current: I = q/t
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — Current is charge per time

I = q/t. Amperes = Coulombs/second. Current is how many charges per second flow past a point.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K5. Current definition.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The Car Project's motor needs a CURRENT — flowing charge through the motor coil makes it spin. Today's foundation, applied tomorrow as Ohm's law.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap voltage as energy/charge.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: current I = q/t. Units: amperes (A) = Coulombs/second. 1 A is HUGE — it's ~6×10¹⁸ electrons per second. Conventional current direction: + to − (historical; electrons actually flow − to +).
18–30 min

• Worked example: 10 A flowing for 3 s — how much charge? 30 C. 60 C passes in a minute — what current? 1 A. A 9V battery rated at 1000 mAh — how many hours at 1 A continuous draw? (1 hour.)
30–42 min

• Direction of conventional current (+ → −) AND direction of electron motion (− → +). Important for Day 13 (Oersted's right-hand rule).
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 9V battery rated at 500 mAh: how many hours can it provide 0.5 A?" (Use I·t = charge.)
48–50 min

• Marzano on K5.

Prep: battery with mAh ratings shown; reference table (amp = C/s); packet practice; calculator.

Talk moves on conventional vs. electron current: when students get confused, stop. "There are two conventions. We use 'conventional' (+ → −) for circuit math. Just know they're opposite to electron flow."

Differentiation: ELs — water-flow visual showing pressure (V), pipe (R), flow rate (I); SWDs — pre-built I·t = q practice; advanced — "explain mAh in terms of Coulombs. (mAh is just an energy-related unit — it's charge.)"

title: Day 6 · Phase 2 — Ohm's law: V = IR
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 2 — Resistance + the central equation

V = IR. Resistance in Ohms (Ω). The triangle that EVERY circuit problem leans on.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K6. Ohm's law. S2. Compute V = IR.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The Car Project's motor has resistance — that determines how much current the 9V battery can push through. Today's V = IR is THE equation for Unit 8.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap voltage + current. What CONNECTS them?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: V = IR. R = resistance, measured in Ohms (Ω) = V/A. Big R = less current for same voltage. Small R = more current. Wire = ~0 Ω. Light bulb filament = a few Ω. A 1-kΩ resistor restricts current heavily.
18–32 min

• Worked examples: (1) 9V across a 100 Ω resistor — I = ? (90 mA) (2) A bulb with 2 Ω resistance carries 6 A — what voltage drove it? (12V) (3) 5 mA through a component when 1.5V across — resistance? (300 Ω)
32–42 min

• Pattern: Ohm's law triangle (V on top, I and R on bottom). Cover V to find V = IR. Cover I to find I = V/R. Cover R to find R = V/I. Master this triangle.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A circuit has 12V across a 4 Ω resistor. What's the current?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K6/S2.

Prep: Ohm's law triangle for the board; packet practice; calculator availability; sample resistors for prop.

Talk moves on the triangle: have students PRACTICE rearranging out loud. "Voltage = current × resistance. Current = voltage / resistance. Resistance = voltage / current."

Differentiation: ELs — Ohm's law triangle on a card; SWDs — pre-built table for the three forms; advanced — "why does a thin wire have MORE resistance than a thick wire of the same material? Hint: cross-sectional area."

title: Day 7 · Phase 3 — Series circuits
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 3 — One loop, all current the same

Series: components share the same current. Voltages ADD. Resistances ADD.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K7. Series circuit rules. S3. Solve series problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The Car Project's circuit IS a series circuit. Battery → switch → motor → battery. Today's rules apply directly on Day 17.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap V = IR.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: in a series circuit (one loop), every component sees the SAME current. The voltage drops across each component ADD to equal the battery's voltage (Kirchhoff voltage law). Resistances ADD: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...
18–32 min

• Worked examples: (1) 12V battery + 2Ω + 4Ω in series. Total R = 6Ω. Current = 2A. Voltage across each: 4V + 8V = 12V ✓. (2) Three identical bulbs in series at 12V: each gets 4V.
32–42 min

• Group practice — 4 problems. Build series circuits in PhET Circuit Construction Kit. Measure I and V at each component. Confirm rules.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 9V battery is in series with a 2 Ω and a 4 Ω resistor. What's the current? What's the voltage across the 4 Ω?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K7/S3.

Prep: PhET Circuit Construction Kit: DC loaded; packet practice; calculator availability; battery + resistors + bulbs + wires + multimeters for hands-on if available.

Talk moves on "same current": push: "Why is the current the SAME at every point in a series loop? Where would extra charges PILE UP if it weren't?" (Charge conservation — no, it can't.)

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing a one-loop series with current arrows; SWDs — pre-built I/V worksheet for each step; advanced — "if one bulb in a series string burns out, what happens to the others? Why?"

title: Day 8 · Phase 3 — Parallel circuits
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 3 — Multiple paths, voltage shared

Parallel: branches share the same voltage. Currents ADD. 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ...

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K7. Parallel circuit rules. S3. Solve parallel problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

House wiring is parallel — that's why one light burning out doesn't take down the whole house. Day 17 contrast: Car Project is series — one wire break stops everything.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: series rules.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: parallel = multiple paths. Each branch sees the SAME voltage (= battery voltage). Currents in each branch ADD to total. Resistances: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ...
18–32 min

• Worked example: 12V across two parallel 6Ω resistors. Each branch carries 12/6 = 2A. Total = 4A. R_total = 12V/4A = 3Ω (matches 1/R_total = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3).
32–42 min

• Group practice — 4 parallel problems. Use PhET. Note: ADDING a parallel branch DECREASES total resistance (more paths for current).
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Three 6 Ω resistors in parallel — what's the total resistance?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K7/S3.

Prep: PhET; packet practice; reference card (series + parallel rules side-by-side); calculator availability.

Talk moves on "why R goes down": push: "If you add ANOTHER path for current to flow, does that make it harder or easier overall? What does that say about total R?"

Differentiation: ELs — side-by-side series vs. parallel diagrams with rules listed; SWDs — pre-built worksheet with the 1/R formula; advanced — "in parallel, ADDING a resistor makes total R DECREASE. Reconcile with the formula and check by computing R for 1, 2, 3 identical resistors in parallel."

title: Day 9 · Phase 3 — Combination circuits
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 3 — Series + parallel together

Reduce step by step. Combine parallel groups, then series, then solve V = IR.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S3. Solve combination circuits.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Most real circuits are combinations. Building muscle for the Vernier lab on Day 10.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: series AND parallel rules.
5–18 min

• Worked example: 12V battery with a 4Ω in series with a parallel combination of 6Ω + 3Ω. Reduce parallel first: 1/R = 1/6 + 1/3 = 1/2 → R = 2Ω. Then series: 4 + 2 = 6Ω total. Current from battery: 12/6 = 2A.
18–32 min

• Find branch currents: 2A enters the parallel section. Voltage across parallel = 2A·2Ω = 4V. Current through 6Ω = 4/6 = 0.67A. Through 3Ω = 4/3 = 1.33A. Sum = 2A ✓.
32–42 min

• Group practice — 3 combination problems. Step by step: reduce, find total, find currents.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 9V battery with a 3Ω in series with a parallel 6Ω + 6Ω. What's the total resistance? What's the battery current?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on S3.

Prep: PhET; combination-circuit worksheets; calculator availability; reference card with steps (reduce parallel → reduce series → solve total → walk back through to branch currents).

Talk moves on the reduction strategy: when students try to compute the WHOLE thing at once, stop. "First reduce ANY parallel groups to a single equivalent R. Then add series. Then use V = IR for the whole thing. THEN work backward to branches."

Differentiation: ELs — circuit-diagram template with reduction stages drawn; SWDs — step-numbered worksheet; advanced — "draw a circuit with TWO parallel groups in series with one resistor. Solve."

title: Day 10 · Phase 3 — Vernier circuit lab: verify Ohm's law + circuit rules
bodyHtml:
DAY 10 · Phase 3 — Empirical confirmation

Build circuits. Measure V and I with Vernier sensors. Verify V = IR; verify series + parallel rules.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S5. Vernier voltmeter + ammeter use. K6/K7. Verify Ohm's law + circuit rules.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If V = IR + series/parallel rules hold in our lab, we trust them for the Car Project. Today's lab is the foundation for Unit 8 wiring.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Lab overview + safety beat (no shorts, low-voltage only, careful with connections).
5–18 min

• Teacher demo: 6V battery + a known resistor (try 100Ω). Use Vernier voltmeter + ammeter (or DMM if available). Measure V across, I through. Compute V/I = R. Should match.
18–38 min

• Lab: pairs run 3 builds. (1) Single resistor — confirm V = IR. (2) Two in series — confirm V_total = V₁ + V₂, I same throughout. (3) Two in parallel — confirm I_total = I₁ + I₂, V same across both.
38–43 min

• Class data compilation. % differences. Discuss error sources: resistor tolerance, ammeter resistance, connection contact.
43–48 min

• Lab write-up start.
48–50 min

• Marzano on S5.

Prep: Vernier voltage + current sensors + LabQuest; 6V battery packs or low-voltage power supplies; resistors (~100-1kΩ assortment); wires + alligator clips + breadboards if available; multimeter as backup; lab write-up template.

Talk moves on %-difference: push: "Your nominal resistor is 100Ω. Your measured V/I gives 97Ω. Why?" (Resistor tolerance often 5-10%; ammeter has internal resistance.)

Differentiation: ELs — pair with LoggerPro-fluent partner; SWDs — pre-built circuit diagrams to copy; advanced — "compute the ammeter's own resistance from your data."

title: Day 11 · Phase 4 — Electric power: P = VI
bodyHtml:
DAY 11 · Phase 4 — How fast does the circuit USE energy?

P = VI. Also P = I²R and P = V²/R. Watts in any electrical context.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K8. Electric power. S4. Compute power.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

How much power does the Car Project's motor consume? P = VI. With a 9V battery and an estimated current, we can answer in advance.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: voltage = energy/charge. Current = charge/time. What's energy/time?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: P = V × I. Voltage × current = (J/C) × (C/s) = J/s = watts. From Ohm's law, P = I²R = V²/R. Three equivalent forms; choose whichever fits your knowns.
18–30 min

• Worked examples: (1) 100W bulb at 120V: I = 100/120 = 0.83A. R = V²/P = 144Ω. (2) Car Project's motor at 9V, ~0.5A: P = 4.5W. (3) Toaster pulling 8A at 120V: P = 960W.
30–42 min

• Energy usage: a 100W bulb on for 10 hours uses 1 kWh. That's what you're billed for. 1 kWh ≈ 3.6×10⁶ J.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A heater draws 12 A at 240V. What's its power? What energy does it use in 2 hours?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K8/S4.

Prep: packet practice; energy-bill or kWh reference; calculator availability.

Talk moves on the three forms: push: "If I know voltage and current, use P=VI. If I know current and resistance only, use P=I²R. If voltage and resistance only, use P=V²/R. Just pick the form that uses what you HAVE."

Differentiation: ELs — three-equation power card; SWDs — table for each form with example; advanced — "why is electricity transmitted at HIGH voltage but used at LOW voltage? Hint: power lost in wires = I²R."

title: Day 12 · Phase 4 — Magnetism: poles, fields, Earth
bodyHtml:
DAY 12 · Phase 4 — The OTHER fundamental field

Magnetic poles always come in pairs. Field lines from N to S. Earth's field comes from its iron core.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K9. Magnetic field + pole behavior.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Earth's magnetic field DEFLECTS charged particles from the Sun (solar wind). It's the magnetosphere — the shield that protects life. We'll explore impact disruption on Day 16.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: bar magnets. Try to push two NORTH poles together. Then north + south. Why?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: magnetic poles come in pairs (N and S). LIKE poles REPEL; UNLIKE attract — same rule as electricity, but here you can't isolate a single pole. Cut a magnet in half — get TWO complete magnets, each with its own N and S.
18–30 min

• Magnetic field B: arrows from N to S outside the magnet, inside go S to N (closed loops). Demo: iron filings on paper over a bar magnet. Pattern reveals the field.
30–42 min

• Earth's field: source = iron core dynamo. Magnetic poles near geographic poles. Compass needles align with B-field. Magnetic NORTH is actually pointing toward Earth's SOUTH magnetic pole (and vice versa). The naming is historical.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "You cut a bar magnet in half. What do you get?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K9.

Prep: bar magnets; iron filings + paper; compass; Earth-magnetic-field diagram.

Talk moves on "why no monopoles": push: "In electricity, you can have a single + or − charge. In magnetism you can't. Why? (We don't fully know. It's a fundamental observation.)"

Differentiation: ELs — magnet field-line visual with poles annotated; SWDs — verbal exit ticket option; advanced — "why does a compass deflect when current flows in a nearby wire? Preview Day 13 — Oersted."

title: Day 13 · Phase 4 — Electromagnetism: Oersted's discovery
bodyHtml:
DAY 13 · Phase 4 — Current creates a magnetic field

The 1820 discovery that linked electricity and magnetism forever.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K10. Current → magnetic field (Oersted). Right-hand rule.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Every wire that carries current is also a tiny magnet. This is what makes the Car Project's MOTOR spin — magnetic force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: "What if I told you a wire carrying current could move a compass needle?"
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: in 1820 Hans Oersted noticed a current-carrying wire deflected a compass needle near it. Current creates a magnetic field. Right-hand rule: point your right thumb in the direction of current flow. Your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field around the wire.
18–30 min

• Demo: lay a compass next to a wire connected to a battery. Switch on the circuit; compass deflects. Reverse the polarity; compass swings the other way.
30–42 min

• Coil application: wind a wire into many loops → the magnetic field is concentrated like a bar magnet. Insert iron core → much stronger. This is an electromagnet. Practical applications: cranes lifting cars, MRI machines, motors.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A current flows north in a straight wire. Use the right-hand rule to determine the direction of the magnetic field ABOVE the wire."
48–50 min

• Marzano on K10.

Prep: battery + wire + compass for Oersted demo; coil of wire + iron nail for electromagnet demo; right-hand rule reference card.

Talk moves on the right-hand rule: when students forget which hand, push: "It's the RIGHT hand. Thumb = current direction. Fingers curl = B-field direction."

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing right-hand rule with thumb + curled fingers labeled; SWDs — hands-on practice with a paper compass; advanced — "why does winding the wire into a coil make the field STRONGER?"

title: Day 14 · Phase 5 — Faraday: induction (changing flux → voltage)
bodyHtml:
DAY 14 · Phase 5 — The reverse direction

Changing magnetic field induces a voltage. Foundation for generators, transformers, wireless charging.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K11. Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction (qualitative).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

A large asteroid impact ionizes the atmosphere. Charged debris moving fast creates a CHANGING magnetic environment — which induces voltages in nearby electronics. That's an EMP. Today's foundation; Day 16's payoff.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap Oersted: current makes B-field. Today: REVERSE — changing B-field makes current.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: Faraday's law — when the magnetic field through a loop CHANGES, a voltage is induced. The faster the change, the bigger the voltage. Move a magnet INTO a coil → current flows. Pull it OUT → current reverses. Stationary magnet → no current.
18–30 min

• Demo: coil + multimeter (or galvanometer). Move a bar magnet in and out. Watch the meter deflect.
30–42 min

• Applications: generators (mechanical motion → electrical energy). Transformers (changing currents → induced voltage). Wireless charging (oscillating magnetic field → AC in receiver coil).
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why does pulling a magnet OUT of a coil produce current in the OPPOSITE direction from pushing it IN?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K11.

Prep: coil + bar magnet + multimeter (or galvanometer); video of generator if no hardware; transformer image.

Talk moves on "why does direction matter": push: "The induced current OPPOSES the change. When magnet pushes in, induced current makes a B-field PUSHING BACK. When pulled out, induced current PULLS IT BACK. (Lenz's law.) Why? Energy conservation."

Differentiation: ELs — diagram showing magnet-into-coil with induced current direction; SWDs — verbal exit ticket option; advanced — "why doesn't a stationary magnet in a coil produce a current, even though there's a magnetic field?"

title: Day 15 · Phase 5 — Motors + generators: the Car Project's heart
bodyHtml:
DAY 15 · Phase 5 — Direct bridge to Unit 8

Motor: electrical energy → mechanical motion. Generator: mechanical motion → electrical energy. Same hardware, run in reverse.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K12. Motor + generator principles.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Today's content IS the Car Project's motor. Unit 8 takes this knowledge and builds.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: "How does the Car Project's motor work? Today."
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: motor: a current-carrying coil in a magnetic field experiences a torque. The coil rotates. A commutator reverses the current every half-turn to keep rotation in one direction. Result: electrical energy → rotational mechanical energy. EXACTLY the DC motor in your kit.
18–30 min

• Generator (reverse): rotate a coil in a magnetic field by hand. Changing B-field through coil INDUCES current (Faraday). Mechanical energy → electrical energy. Power plants use steam to spin generators.
30–42 min

• Demo: simple motor (battery + coil + magnet + paper clip). OR generator (hand-crank generator if available). Discuss the energy chain explicitly: chemical → electrical → mechanical-rotational.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why does the Car Project's motor SPIN when you connect the 9V battery? Explain in terms of forces on the wire."
48–50 min

• Marzano on K12.

Prep: small DC motor (the actual Car Project motor, if available); battery + coil + magnet for simple-motor demo; hand-crank generator if available; energy-chain reference.

Talk moves on the energy chain: push: "Walk through the FULL chain: chemical in battery → electrical in wires → mechanical-rotational in the motor shaft → mechanical-translational of the car. Where is each form?"

Differentiation: ELs — motor schematic with each part labeled; SWDs — verbal version of exit ticket; advanced — "why is your motor LESS efficient than the theoretical max? What does that say about efficiency?"

title: Day 16 · Phase 6 — Asteroid impact: EMP + magnetosphere
bodyHtml:
DAY 16 · Phase 6 — Planetary-scale E&M

The magnetosphere protects us. A large impact briefly disrupts it. Bonus: EMP from ionized debris.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R4. Reason about cosmic + impact E&M phenomena.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Earth's magnetosphere protects life by deflecting solar wind. An asteroid impact ionizes the atmosphere — creating a brief but powerful electromagnetic disturbance. Plus: the magnetic field has measurable changes from past large impacts.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame: "Earth has its own magnetic field. Why does it matter for our story?"
5–18 min

• Magnetosphere: Earth's iron-core-generated magnetic field deflects most cosmic and solar charged particles. Aurorae happen at the poles where particles funnel in. Mars LOST its magnetosphere (cold core) and lost most of its atmosphere as a result.
18–32 min

• Impact EMP: when an asteroid impacts, the fireball ionizes air around it. Ionized gas in motion = HUGE electric currents. Those currents generate a brief magnetic pulse — an electromagnetic pulse (EMP) — that can induce damaging voltages in electronics far from the impact (via Faraday's law).
32–42 min

• Discussion: which technology is most vulnerable? Power grids, satellites, GPS. Why? Long conductors that pick up induced voltages efficiently.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why does Earth still have an atmosphere while Mars mostly doesn't?" (Magnetosphere.)
48–50 min

• Marzano on R4.

Prep: magnetosphere diagram; aurora image; reference to nuclear-test EMP history.

Talk moves on "why ionized gas matters": push: "Plasma is HOT charged particles — they're moving carriers of charge. Moving charge = current. Current = magnetic field."

Differentiation: ELs — visual of magnetosphere deflecting solar wind; SWDs — pre-printed cause-effect chart for EMP; advanced — "why are SHORT electrical wires less vulnerable to EMP than LONG ones?"

title: Day 17 · Phase 6 — Bridge to Unit 8: the Car Project Preview
bodyHtml:
DAY 17 · Phase 6 — Where Unit 7 lands

Preview the Car Project. Every piece of Unit 7 maps onto a build decision.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 7 targets surfaced through the Car Project preview.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The year's asteroid framework officially closes today. Tomorrow's transfer task assesses Unit 7; the next 9 class periods are Unit 8 — students BUILD what they've learned.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame the day. "Today is the bridge. Unit 7 + Unit 8 — how they connect."
5–18 min

• The Car Project preview. Show the parts kit. Map each part to Unit 7 content: battery (Day 4 voltage), wires (Day 6 conduction/resistance), switch (Day 6 series-circuit gate), motor (Day 15 current-in-field-makes-torque), and the SERIES circuit topology (Day 7).
18–32 min

• Predict-then-test: given a 9V battery and the motor's resistance (~3-5 Ω), what CURRENT will flow? Then: what POWER does the motor consume? (P = VI ≈ 15-25 W typical, brief.) Class predicts numbers; they'll measure on Day 6 of the Car Project.
32–42 min

• Wheel + gear ratio reminder from Unit 2's mechanical advantage: how does gear ratio convert motor RPM to axle RPM? Tease the Day 3 of Car Project content.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why does the Car Project use a SERIES circuit instead of parallel?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on full unit target list.

Prep: Car Project parts kit on the table; circuit diagram for Day 17 preview; reference card showing each part to its Unit 7 origin.

Talk moves on the series choice: push: "In a series circuit, if any component fails, all current stops. Is that a feature or a bug for a car? (Feature — safety: switch can stop everything.)"

Differentiation: ELs — visual mapping each kit piece to its Unit 7 day; SWDs — pre-printed comparison table; advanced — "could you build the Car Project using a PARALLEL circuit? What would that look like and what would it do?"

title: Day 18 · Phase 6 — Unit 7 transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 18 · Phase 6 — Assessment of learning

5 multi-part problems integrating charges, Ohm's law, series + parallel, power, magnetism, and the Car Project bridge.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 7 targets, surfaced through transfer problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The transfer task includes a Car Project circuit analysis (R2/S3) as the load-bearing prompt — making explicit that Unit 7's learning gates Unit 8's build.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Brief reminder of the 4-dimension rubric (Identify / Calculate / Construct / Transfer). Lowest-dimension grading.
5–45 min

• Transfer task. Independent. 5 multi-part problems: (1) Coulomb's law — F between two charges; (2) Ohm's law on a 6V battery + resistor; (3) combination circuit reduction + branch currents; (4) electric power + energy (kWh); (5) Car Project circuit — given 9V battery and motor's estimated resistance, compute current, power, and total charge over 30 seconds of operation.
45–48 min

• Final Marzano on the unit target list.
48–50 min

• Growth chart updated. Unit 8 — The Car Project — begins tomorrow.

Prep: transfer task printed; calculators; constants reference card (k, conventions); quiet testing environment; growth chart pages.

Differentiation on the transfer task: ELs — vocabulary glossary attached (charge, current, voltage, resistance, series, parallel, magnetism); SWDs — option to verbal-explain dimension 4 (Transfer) of any problem; advanced — bonus prompt: "Use Unit 7 thinking to predict the speed of the Car Project's wheels given motor RPM and gear ratio."

Closing: Growth chart picked up tomorrow at start of Unit 8 — the build year-end capstone where electricity, mechanical advantage, and engineering execution all come together.
